© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
flows a little bit with conditions 	but it never really goes away 	– 	and “End of
America,” 	a huge hit.
Against an enemy 	– 	an 	evergreen category. As 	long as somebody has an enemy,
there will be stuff they buy because of that enemy.
With the “$4,000 	In Your Underwear” 	ad, 	we got a big bump as soon as we put a
paragraph at the bottom 	about 	Hillary coming to get your money. 	Now it would
be about Obama coming to get your money and 	in however many years’ time,
when whoever it is takes 	that 	place, 	it will be about that person coming to get
your money. 	And it 	will still get the bump.
So 	enemy and then 	clever combinations thereof.
This, 	by 	the 	way, 	is 	the 	current 	“End 	of
America” 	piece so this is the same pitch.
I just want to show you how little it takes to
update anything. This is the same 	End of
America pitch that Stansberry has now used
for three years 	– 	the 	direct mail piece.
I want to show you the changes that 	make 	it
current.
One, he has 	picked a new date for the 	End of
America because, 	lo 	and behold, 	it didn’t end
on the last three dates.
There’s a new sticker on it 	– 	which 	really isn’t a sticker, 	it’s just printed to look
like a sticker, it’s 	got a picture of Obama 	– 	our 	“enemy.”
It says, “New Obama 	Currency Law Goes Into Effect 	July 	1st” 	and it appears in
two places.
The 	rest of this 	is unchanged; 	same pitch. It 	hasn’t changed since 	the 	beginning.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 18 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	19
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
They’re 	still mailing the 	crap out of it and it’s still working.
So 	those are your 	categories of evergreen appeals and you 	ought to 	consider
them when you go build any presentation.
You 	might as well build it on a foundation of 	one of the appeals that never dies,
that is reliable when you structure a presentation.
The Lost Secret of 7x to 21x
I call this the lost secret of 	7 	to 21 	times. We 	used to get this; 	everybody in my
business understood it about the training side of our business.
We always 	sold 	with it too; 	the 	7 	to 21 times repetition.
If 	you move into a new house, 	like an idiot… 	this was 	part of the pitch to sell
cassette programs… 	because you 	were going to 	go 	listen to them 	7 	to 21 times;
then you did a 	value proposition by the way, so there 	are 	six 	cassettes 	in an
album but you have to listen to each one seven to 21 times.
So, even if you only do seven, 	that’s 42. If 	you divide
$60 by 42, 	it’s a lot less than it is if you divide $60 by
six so it’s part of the value proposition.
The 	reason that’s important 	is because, 	if you move
into a new house, 	then depending upon how quick 	a
study you are 	but, at least for seven nights, 	like an
idiot, 	you will be in the dark reaching for the light
switches where they were in the old house.
Most people will 	be reaching for the light 	switches
where they were in the old house for 21 nights in a row 	before their hand and
mind readjust and automatically go to the light switch where it is in the new
house.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 19 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	20
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
Therefore you need seven to 21 repetitions of any input that you want to
actually stick and be held and be able to be used; 	so that was the pitch. We all
understood it.
What most people don’t know is not only 	was it a good pitch, 	it’s legitimate; it’s
actually true.
That’s 	not necessary from the standpoint of a 	pitch 	but it is 	actually true. It is
how we get things, 	we get them through repetition, and we get them even when
we don’t try.
Everybody in the room knows the lyrics to some song 	you made no attempt
whatsoever to memorize; 	it’s not like you sat
there and 	tried to memorize it, but you know
them 	and 	at 	least 	with 	a 	prompt 	you 	can
probably 	do them.
It’s why 	“Name That Tune” 	works. 	You 	can hear
the first few bars of 	a 	song and you know what
the song is. You didn’t try and do that; 	it happened to you automatically through
repetition.
It happens automatically by accident without you trying to do it if you just apply
it to what you’re trying to do.
This 	is 	how I got through Latin in 	high school. By 	the way, 	the first dumb
mistake 	was 	taking Latin, some 	vague 	thought that I wanted to be a veterinarian
and at the time you still needed 	Latin to get through vet school or medical
school.
It was not that 	I 	was planning 	on 	being 	a priest, 	I was planning on being a
veterinarian. We 	had Spanish, French and Latin and the hottest teacher was
Latin. There was two means of making that decision, 	it turned 	out 	to be
completely useless, every once in a while I can get 	Quo Vadis 	into a conversation
but…
You 	need 	7 	to 21
repetitions of any
input that you want
to actually stick.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 20 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	21
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
Here’s how 	I 	got through Latin. Our 	system anyway, 	you got a weekly quiz and
you got a monthly test. The weekly quiz was vocabulary 	– 	English word, 	Latin
word; 	Latin word, 	English word 	– 	it 	can be memorized. 	The 	monthly test you
had to use 	the 	words in present tense, 	past tense, 	and 	future tense in sentences.
For the grade, 	quizzes counted for half the grade, 	tests 	counted for half the
grade. 	That 	means, 	if you aced the quizzes, 	you could flunk the test and get a C 	–
that’s me.
The memorization was recording the vocabulary and then playing it on an audio
cassette over and over and over again until the quiz, 	including while I slept 	with
a 	little pillow speaker.
Aced every single quiz, 	flunked every single test. I 	just sat there because I had no
idea. Three 	or four times, I was accused of 	cheating on the quizzes because how
could you ace the quiz and flunk the test?
I kept explaining to them because, 	to do the test, 	you have to be able to use this
crap; 	to ace the quiz you only have to know it. 	I know it, 	I memorized it; 	I just
can’t use it.
Our minds really do work this way 	– 	stuff 	sticks 	through repetition.
Repetition and Reinforcement
So, 	when you build a presentation, 	you want seven to 21 repetitions 	of the most
important things in them internally during the course of the presentation.
If you go 	through one of my scripts 	and you 	hit upon a big idea 	– 	an 	important
point made early 	– 	and now you stick count how many times that’s restated
almost identically during the course of that presentation, 	you’re going to get
seven to 21.
I don’t miss because I make sure I don’t miss.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 21 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	22
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
So 	how do you say it seven to
21 	times 	in 	a 	presentation
without being 	horribly clumsy
about it? 	Although 	it’s 	better to
be horribly clumsy about it than
not do it.
One, 	you build it into your very
Open 	– 	you 	build it into your
Open tease, 	your Open promise,
your 	Open assertions.
As 	early in the presentation as
you possibly 	can, you put the most important thing you want to drive home that
has to be accepted in order for them to buy.
You can say it by direct assertion. 	You 	can bury it inside a demonstration and
have an opportunity to say it again, 	show it, 	prove it.
You 	can bury it inside testimonials. The testimonials either say it or they get
close to it or they reinforce it. So 	you can deliver it inside testimonials.
You 	can put it inside a story. You can put it inside the headline of a story. 	So the
way you announce the 	story or start a story allows you to say it again.
If 	our big idea is 	“Big Money in Big Chunks,” 	we can put it in every place I’ve
named so far.
We can put it in an opening tease, 	we can say it as direct assertion, 	“With this
system, 	you’ll get big money 	in 	big chunks.”
We can put it in some kind of demonstration 	– 	we 	could have 	the 	checks you
take 	out of the envelopes 	in the video 	being 	$5,000 checks instead of $19 checks
and we’ve demonstrated big money in big chunks.
Repetition & Reinforcement of Promise(s):
How 	to Say Them 7-21 Times in 	a
Presentation 	Without Being Horribly Clumsy
1. 	Opening “Teases”
2. 	Direct Assertion
3. 	Inside 	Demonstration(s)
4. 	Inside Testimonial(s)
5. 	Inside Stories
6. 	As Headline of Story
7. 	As Punch Line of Story
8. 	Tied 	to a 	Proffer 	of Fact
9. 	Inside the Guarantee
10. 	As 	a 	Trial Close
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 22 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	23
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
We can have 	a 	testimonial say, “I 	thought only rich people and doctors and
lawyers got big money in big chunks 	but now…” So 	we have a 	testimonial 	say it.
The 	headline of the 	story 	says, “Here’s the story of a desperate Iowa 	housewife
who discovered how to get big money in big chunks.” 	Then we tell her story.
As 	the punch line of the story 	– 	at 	the end, 	“As you can see, 	Judy discovered how
to get big money in big chunks.”
We 	can tie it to a proffer of facts. If our big idea is 	“Get your teeth three shades
whiter 	in just three days,” 	we can tie it to the facts; 	so, 	“The Harvard 	study
showed 	that nine out of 10 dentists 	recommend 	X, 	which is exactly why you can
rely on 	X 	to get yours 	three shades whiter in just three days.”
They didn’t say you could… but, if we 	say 	“it’s 	exactly why…” 	everybody says,
“Okay, that’s exactly why.”
We 	can obviously put it inside the guarantee. 	“If you don’t get big money in big
chunks within 89 days, 	you can return everything.”
So 	we can put it in 	a 	guarantee and we can stick it in a trial 	close.
So there’s 10 ways we can try and get our 	repetition within the presentation and
it’s not the same as just 	repeating 	it over and over again.
Now One to Many at a 	distance and even in a room is handicapped by the fact
that often you are speed selling and they do 	not have a firm grip on 	what it is
they are buying. 	There is an impulse to the purchase.
I had a guy who, 	over the years at the 	Success events, 	bought 	Magnetic
Marketing nine times. 	He didn’t realize it until the 10th year and he’s telling me,
“I bought it 	in 1996, 	I bought it in 1998, 	I bought it…”
He 	was moving 	and finally it all came together, 	all still shrink wrapped of course.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 23 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	24
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
He 	just 	got 	caught up in the pitch and ran to 	the back of the room and bought it,
never once thinking, “I was here three years ago 	– 	same guy, 	same suit, 	same
slides 	– 	I 	wonder if it’s the same product.”
We’re speed selling; 	they’re buying on impulse; 	they often don’t know exactly
what they’re buying. 	That’s somewhat of a handicap, so we’ve 	got to implant the
big idea that drives the purchase through repetition.
There’s 	also a thing about it being 	a closed loop. There is a 	book 	‘Sham’ 	by Steve
Salerno 	– 	and 	I will preface 	this 	by saying it’s only about 70% right and in places
it is profoundly unfair 	but 	still really everybody ought to read it 	– 	just 	substitute
what goes on in your industry to this.
If you are in any 	way shape or form my industry that’s 	what it’s written about. 	It
is an attack on the 	self-help industry that’s what it is.
It’s a little 	dated now but Tony’s 	in it, 	Barbara 	De
Angelis is in it and John Gray 	– 	the Venus/Mars
guy 	– 	is 	in it. So 	there’s a lot of people that you
know who are critically featured in the book.
SHAM 	stands 	for 	self-help 	and 	actualization
movement. 	The reason 	to read it 	beyond fun 	– 	if
you have sort of a perverse idea of fun 	– 	is 	that
he does accurately reveal a lot of 	the structural stuff about 	how 	self-help,
motivation 	and 	success is sold to people.
His background 	is he 	was at Rodale 	for many years as a copywriter. You can get
the book at Amazon.
One of the things that he reveals is there 	is a circular closed 	loop to this
structure.
We’ve got to implant
the big idea that
drives the purchase
through repetition.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 24 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	25
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
This is his 	criticism:
This 	is not 	to say it’s false. There are 	self-help 	precepts 	that can’t be disputed 	–
BECAUSE THEY ARE CIRCULAR 	– 	the 	guru is saying the same thing in different
ways at the start and end of the sentence. The conclusion restates the promise.
If 	you start to get analytical about this, 	you will see it done again and again.
The conclusion proves the promise and the conclusion is only a restatement of
the promise.
Here’s a Sham 	example:
Incredibly, 	most sales professionals choose to be miserable while 	a 	few choose to
prosper and enjoy selling.
Now jump to the end, 	I won’t read the middle 	first:
That fact guarantees that you can choose freedom from prospecting or drudgery
while others 	choose misery.
It’s a big circle. 	We’re just going round in circles. Now 	I’ll 	read the whole thing:
Incredibly, 	most sales professionals choose to be miserable while 	a 	few choose 	to
prosper and enjoy selling.
There’s nothing more discouraging to sales professionals 	than endless, 	difficult
cold prospecting.
Most 	simply resign themselves to it. 	But 	those who discover 	Magnetic 	Marketing
break free of it and never waste a minute 	being an annoying pest 	instead of a
welcome guest again.
That’s how 	you too 	can transform your experience in selling.
That fact… 	[What fact? Be analytical for a second, 	what fact?]
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 25 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	26
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
That fact guarantees that you can choose freedom from 	prospecting drudgery
while others 	choose misery.
…which is a restatement of the first 	sentence 	of the paragraph.
It’s a self-proving circular piece of language that didn’t go anywhere, and didn’t
really 	establish anything.
When you build your seven to 21 repetitions, 	you can build them as closed
loops, which actually gets you another repetition because you are proving the
promise with a restatement of the promise and you’re calling the middle that
said 	nothing the fact that proves the promise.
Methods of Reinforcement
When 	you 	go 	to 	repeat 	and
reinforce, 	you can do it verbally
so somehow it’s done verbally.
You 	can do it visually 	– 	live 	or
video 	– 	and 	you can do it in
print if you have print.
By the way, 	I’ve 	got a little lazy
about it over the years but it’s
amazing how many order 	forms
don’t have any testimonials on
them, 	particularly 	speakers
who use them live.
My 	Peter 	Lowe 	form 	had
testimonials on the back.
If 	they’re going to stand in line
waiting 	and 	they’re 	thinking
and 	maybe 	they’re 	going 	to
walk away, 	they might as well
Repetition & Reinforcement of Promise(s):
How 	to Say Them 7-21 Times in 	a
Presentation 	Without Being Horribly Clumsy
REINFORCED:
VERBAL
 	Said by Presenter 	– 	“Company Song”
 	Said by 	Others 	– 	“In Unison”
VISUAL
 	Pennant, Sign, Backdrop, Product
Package
 	Product Beauty Shots
 	Represented by Place, Symbolism,
etc.
PRINTED
 	Pennant, Sign, Backdrop, Product
Package
 	Type On Screen
 	On Order Form
 	Print Sent With CD or DVD
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 26 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	27
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
read 	a 	testimonial and the testimonials 	reinforce the key promises.
Rules of Reinforcement
There’s 	a 	few rules, 	sort of.
One 	is you want to bookend one way or another. Because the law 	of “primacy
and recency” 	says, 	most people if you bring them up on stage and you give them
a list 	of 	six random numbers 	– 	1, 48, 12, 9… 	– 	most 	people can remember the
first couple and the last couple 	but 	they can’t get the ones in the middle. And 	if
they get the ones in the middle they 	get them in the wrong order.
It’s called primacy and recency and the longer the presentation the more
problematic this is, because they can only remember what you started with and
what you’re ending with and they can’t remember anything in the middle.
It’s even true of a short paragraph so you want 	to 	bookend and bookend 	and
bookend 	when you do your repetition.
Sometimes you’re going to have
to 	stop 	and 	summarize 	at
points 	within 	a 	long
presentation. 	In 	my 	Magnetic
Marketing speech, 	I have three
acts; 	I had three things that we
talked about 	– 	message, market
and 	media.
There’s a summary statement,
“Okay now we’ve got message
taken care of, now we 	will…”
“Now we have 	message 	and 	market, so 	now we will go 	to…”
So 	you have to stop and put what you’ve done together for them.
Repetition & Reinforcement of Promise(s):
How 	to Say Them 7-21 Times in 	a
Presentation 	Without Being Horribly Clumsy
A Few Rules
 	Bookend 	– 	Primacy & Recency
 	Longer the presentation, the more
you need “stop & summarize” at
points within the presentation
 	Open & close, start & stop one
secondary promise at a time + always
connect 	each one to the primary
promise
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 27 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	28
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
Now I got all of those things as a 	primacy. 	“Now 	he’s going to talk again and he’s
going to stop with a point and so I’ll remember this and I can remember where
he winds up. I’m just kind of riding along in between.”
Open 	and 	close, 	start and stop one secondary promise at a time, 	always
connecting them back to the primary promise.
Because if you do four 	or 	five secondaries, 	they 	forgot the primary, if you don’t
keep making that hook.
Mistakes 	Most Often Made with the Middle
The mistakes most often made in the 	Middle are, 	number one, the whole thing is
just 	too damn complicated.
The 	people that make this mistake the most are people
who, 	for one reason or another, 	feel compelled to teach.
They have convinced themselves, 	for one reason or another, “These people
came to a seminar, so we should give them a seminar.”
They came to a seminar but you’re doing 	a sales 	presentation.
Those 	are two entirely different things.
They 	came 	to 	an 	interview 	as 	a 	webcast 	yes, 	but 	you’re 	doing 	a 	sales
presentation.
Teachers 	– 	people who like to teach, 	feel some ethical guilt-ridden 	need to teach
for 	whatever reason 	– 	get 	themselves in real trouble 	because they make the
whole thing way too complicated than anybody is ready for at their first
exposure.
Phill 	Grove, 	who features in one of 	my video sales letters, 	is an 	engineer 	by
background; 	so he thinks like an engineer and 	he 	sounds like an engineer.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 28 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	29
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
That’s reassuring to a lot of people. 	I like that part of him. 	But, turned loose in a
preview seminar, 	he teaches 	and he teaches 	all 	the complicated stuff. 	37 ways to
make a deal 	– 	he 	tries to teach all 37 of them.
I’m going, “Phill, 	they can’t take it. That’s 	a 	years’ worth of coaching. They just
want to know that you’ve 	got 37 ways and they can get a deal. We 	don’t need to
teach 	it, 	it’s in the way.”
You can make it way too complicated.
People always ask me would I
do it differently today? In the
whole 	Magnetic 	Marketing
speech, 	once we get to media,
the 	only 	example 	I 	show 	is
direct mail and mostly I talk
about direct mail.
There’s a reason for that 	– 	they
can’t handle more than one media.
If 	I start talking to them about direct mail and email and broadcast fax 	and
everything, I just made it way too complicated to make the sale.
It’s like why three steps? There’s no magic in the three steps; 	it can be five, 	it can
be 	forty-seven47. But 	three is a nice number. 	People 	can remember three. 	I have
a whole framework of three in that speech.
Message, market, media; 	three-step 	letter; 	there’s a whole series of threes.
People can remember threes.
It 	also works with the Dunning letter story 	– 	the 	collection industry story 	–
which 	is three steps but not everybody uses three steps.
Mistakes Most Often Made with the Middle
 	Too complicated 	– 	too many ideas,
too much information
 	L/V gets “lost”
 	Too much information 	– 	not enough
EMOTION
 	No fun
 	Creating doubt rather than
reassurance
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 29 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	30
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
But 	if I 	say, 	“Here’s 	how you do a three-step and then if you want to do a five-
step and you could do a 17-step and you could integrate email and you could
integrate phone calls,” 	I’m not going to sell 	shit.
I sold because they got it, 	“Okay three steps. 	I could probably do three steps. 	It’s
a little taxing but I could probably do three. 	I haven’t run out of fingers; 	when I
count them, 	I’ve got fingers left over. Alright 	I 	kind of get it.”
You can over complicate the 	Middle 	– 	too 	many ideas,
too 	much information, 	too much teaching.
To 	play fair about this, 	for the most part, 	there’s very little that you can do in
one presentation of any length that the overwhelming majority of the people are
actually going to be able to take, go 	and implement and 	get any results for.
You’re smoking crack; 	they can’t do it. I 	know Tony claims he can touch 	them 	on
the head and give them an orgasm and change their lives 	forever. 	God bless him
but, 	two days later, 	they’re right back to where they were.
The 	best 	we 	can 	do is 	we 	can 	create 	awareness 	of 	new 	methods; 	new
opportunities; 	new 	solutions; 	new 	ways 	of
thinking about something.
We can create interest in it and then we can
provide some sort of opportunity to implement
over 	time 	such as buying a toolkit.
From a playing fair standpoint, 	you do no one any
favors by just burying their butt in information.
They won’t go use it anyway. 	They 	can’t.
My theft 	control speaker, 	when I got him as 	a client, I took his one-day seminar
and I made three days out of it. I said, 	“Jack, 	they can’t. They’re lost by the time
you’re 	at 	1:00 in the afternoon.”
We can create
interest and then
provide some sort of
opportunity to
implement.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 30 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	31
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
They get lost 	that’s the second big mistake.
When 	things aren’t hooked together for them 	– 	if 	you 	leave 	it up to them to
make the connections 	– 	they will get lost.
You 	need structure because they need structure.
Too 	much information, 	not enough emotion. 	They 	only need to know enough
to buy. 	The 	rest of the time 	ought to be 	motivating them about buying 	– 	either
by scaring the crap out of them if you wish 	or 	painting them the wonderful
picture of how things will be.
Whether you like it or not, 	if you are a group presenter, 	you are a motivational
speaker. You may not like the term any 	more than 	I 	like the term but that’s what
you are.
They only need to know enough to buy; 	they don’t need to know any 	more than
that.
You gain no leverage by giving them any 	more knowledge
than 	what they need to buy.
All 	you do is waste time, 	cost yourself money and deprive them of whatever
opportunity it is that you are presenting to them.
You’ve 	got to get to emotion.
No 	fun 	– 	buying is supposed to be fun. 	People 	shop for fun.
You 	might not, 	I might not. 	But 	Wal-Mart’s 	entire business is because it is an
entertainment for low income people and that’s, 	by the way, 	why their sales
right now have taken a big 	dip.
Because low income people have less discretionary 	money 	– 	the price of gas is
up, the 	price of food is up so a higher percentage of their income is going to
basic necessities. Now they go to 	the 	Wal-Mart store three times a week and
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 31 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	32
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
they actually pretty much only get what they went there to get that they need
and then they go home.
They get toilet paper and they get some frozen pizzas and they get a bottle of
juice and they go home.
Wal-Mart’s whole business is about them coming three times a week to get
those things, 	but 	then being 	there for an hour and a half as their entertainment.
They 	wander through the store, they find a DVD they might want to watch and
they find a new kitchen gadget and then they find… that’s their entertainment.
Shopping for them is fun. 	For 	you, 	going 	to 	Nieman 	Marcus might 	be fun or
going to Nordstrom might be fun. 	For me, 	going to a horse auction is fun. 	I’m
shopping, 	I’m buying; 	I feel good because I bought
and I had fun.
The 	reason Disney sell so much stuff is because
people have fun while they are buying. They have
completely 	integrated 	shopping 	with
entertainment, the whole thing. 	You 	can’t get out of an attraction without going
through 	the store.
You’ve got to go to 	the World 	Showcase part of 	Epcot. 	World Showcase is a
bunch of countries. 	I explain this every once in 	a while to 	Carla, “It would be
ever so nice to go 	to…” I say, “We can go to Orlando 	and 	within half a day we can
do 22 countries. 	How 	many more do you need to visit? We’ll be at the pool in the
afternoon if you want 	to, you can go to a spa you can go get a 	massage. 	We
knocked out Europe, Asia, Africa; 	we blew them all out.”
World Showcase is countries; 	that’s what it is 	– 	little miniature landmarks, 	little
main street village area of Paris and London, Istanbul whatever else.
It’s 	stores. They are 	gift 	shops, 	that’s what they are.
They 	only need to
know enough to buy.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 32 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	33
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
There’s one movie in each one, that’s the only thing that’s there that isn’t about
buying stuff. Then there 	are 	restaurants and there 	are 	stores. 	It’s all imported
stuff from the country that it represents. So 	everything we bought at 	Harrods 	in
London and 	schlepped home 	and brought through customs and all that stuff I
can buy right there in 	Disney 	– 	I’ve proven it 	– 	they’ll ship it to my house and
they’ll throw in 	a 	free Mickey Mouse watch.
The whole thing is shopping. 	They 	have made a day of shopping and they have
people confused. 	They 	don’t realize they’re shopping even. They think they’re
being 	entertained and they’re having a ball and somehow they wind up 	with 	18
bags full of stuff, because that’s what it is.
It’s ingenious. 	It’s a mall that nobody really thinks of as a mall 	– 	that’s what it is,
it’s clever.
People buy more happily when they have some fun doing it.
So 	your webcast, 	your webinar, 	your meeting ought not be too serious from
beginning to end. It’s okay to have a 	little fun with people. If they’re smart
customers, 	it’s even okay to let them have a little inside baseball, to let them
understand a little bit about what’s happening to them. 	So there ought to be fun.
Another 	mistake is that they actually tell things and tell too much about things
that they 	raise doubt rather than create reassurance.
When Phill 	teaches the 37 ways that you can get a deal, 	he isn’t reassuring the
people we’ve put in the room; 	he’s raising a bunch of doubts in the people we
put in the room.
Because now it sounds really complicated and, “How 	am 	I going 	to 	pick which
ones; 	do I have to do all of them?” 	And 	all the technical language comes out of
his mouth 	– 	appraisals and property evaluations and lenders and federal law
1803/xyz42 that was passed 	and 	covers land trusts.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 33 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	34
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
Everybody is going, “Wait a minute, 	I thought this was big money in big chunks.
This don’t 	sound like big money 	in 	big chunks to me. 	This 	guy 	is a rocket
scientist.”
We 	need to reassure people. 	They 	need to feel ever more secure that they can do
this; 	that it’s painless, that you can figure it out; 	that you’re going to have all 	this
help.
So a big mistake is actually raising doubt rather than further erasing doubt.
Bridges and Segues
The 	next category has to do with bridges and segues. A lot of 	people ask, “How
do you transition from primary content to selling?”
The 	first part of that answer is you don’t, 	because you should have been selling
the 	entire time. 	The 	idea that 	there is a border…
You’ve 	got to be 	Putin 	here. You’ve 	got to understand 	“no borders”; 	you guys
only thought there was a border.
The 	idea that there is a border somehow between primary
content and selling, 	all that does is get you in trouble.
The worst version of it is 	the 	person who delivers content, content, content 	and
then they sort of physiologically change, and now they deliver a commercial.
Here’s what happens when 	the 	person has a choice… that’s TV 	– 	entertainment,
entertainment, entertainment, 	commercial. What does everybody do when a
commercial comes 	on? 	They go get food, 	they go get a beer, 	they go to the
bathroom to get rid of the food so 	that, 	on the next break, 	they can get 	some
more food.
They’re either in the kitchen or they’re in the can every time the commercials
come on unless they have a way to zoom past them, because they recorded the
show instead 	of 	they’re watching the show live.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 34 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	35
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
Some 	people are too lazy to get up off the couch so 	they sit there 	but the only
time people voluntarily want to see the commercials 	is Super Bowl. There’s a
ton of people who now skip the game 	– 	so 	they yack, 	they make sandwiches,
they go to the can during the game and then, “Oh, 	the commercials are on.”
The last four years I’ve been some place with 	people watching 	Super Bowl 	and
you actually hear it in the house, 	“Hey the commercials are on, 	come in.”
Everybody runs in to watch the commercials. I’m going, “I thought this was a
football game.”
So 	if it doesn’t work for TV, 	why would you do it to yourself?
What happens if you do content, content, content, 	whoops commercial, 	is they
mentally disconnect right then. They go to the mental toilet and they think about
something completely unrelated to what you’ve
been 	talking 	about 	and, 	incidentally, 	start
forgetting all of the points you made at a rapid
pace.
They now go think about sex 	or they think about
the leaky faucet, or they think about whatever;
they mentally leave.
When you stop giving the commercial and you start telling 	a 	story or something,
they 	are 	sort 	of, “He’s back to content.” That’s no good.
Furthermore, 	the 	thought that 	there are boundaries is 	a false 	thought.
Every 	One to 	Many selling presentation is a selling presentation.
Does it have content in it? Yes. Are people capable of confusing it to our
advantage? Yes.
Just like people who spend half a day in the countries 	at 	Disney don’t think that
they’re in a shopping mall, people can see your presentation and think they’re in
a content presentation.
People can see your
presentation and
think they’re in a
content
presentation.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 35 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	36
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
We got two things from my presentations on the 	Success events during 	the nine
years that I did them 	– 	white 	mail 	about this, 	most of which went to Peter first.
We got complaints 	– 	more 	than any other speaker by the way. 	So 	I consistently
held the record in the most 	number of 	people who would take the time to write
a 	letter to complain about something.
I got those 	– 	a 	whole variety but most of them predictable, 	you could guess. 	I
also got the most number of letters complaining essentially about the entire
event 	by praising the fact that, “Finally this guy we never heard of, 	at the end of
the day, 	gave us more content than anybody else all added together.”
I have a big stack of them. 	I used to send the ones that 	specifically 	referenced 	Zig
to him 	– 	once we were buddies 	– 	with 	a 	Post-it Note on it 	saying, “See!”
People 	thought they got more content from my presentation than they got from
any other presentation. Not so 	– 	especially not useable content.
I mean, 	you’ve 	got to be really astute to leave from that 	presentation, 	go home
and do anything. 	You’ve 	got to be sharp as 	a tack.
But, because I did all the stuff we talked about
earlier with ideas that stick, I did numbers; 	I did
all 	the 	stuff 	so 	they 	thought 	they’re 	getting
content.
My goal was to give them 	not one minute of useable content.
I’m there to sell stuff. 	I’m very clear about why I’m there.
I made that mistake my first year of speaking and I got it completely out of my
system. 	I thought if you 	just 	gave them a ton of great content, 	they can clearly
see you have taught them 512 secrets of the meaning of life on this planet, you
can then 	just say, “Over there, 	some of my stuff,” 	and they’ll all run over there
and get it.
I’m there to sell stuff.
I’m 	very clear about
why I’m there.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 36 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	37
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
I made that mistake; a 	lot of people never outgrow that mistake by the way.
They 	keep trying to make the content better, 	thinking 	that 	will cause people to
be smart enough to now buy their stuff. People ain’t 	that smart; 	they’re just not.
All you do with that is defeat you and defeat them.
So 	I made that mistake; 	I made 	the 	mistake of 	trying to do the mix and have the
borders and all that and, in about 	a year, 	I got it all out of my system. I said, “I
don’t care 	– 	90 	minutes, 	four hours, 	eight hours, 	whatever you want me to do 	–
when 	I sit down and build it, 	if I build a new one or if 	I tweak one I’ve got, 	I’m
building a sales presentation that’s what I’m building.”
The main way to do it so people can’t tell the difference is by telling them what
to do and what not to do, but never telling them how to do it 	– 	and 	then just
continuing 	to reinforce, 	show and demonstrate the 	“what to do” 	and the 	“what
not to do.” 	But you never really get to the 	“how to do it.”
So 	if you go to 	the Magnetic 	Marketing speech, 	you see the whole conversation
within message about 	a Unique Selling Proposition; 	why you need 	a Unique
Selling Proposition 	and the fact that nobody has one 	and here’s 	a great example
which is 	Domino’s.
When 	I’m all done, 	here’s what people know 	– 	they 	know they need one 	and
they know they 	ain’t got one.
They 	have no earthly idea how to go sit down with a pad and a pen and build
one; none, 	zero, 	nada.
I didn’t give 	them 	any templates; 	I didn’t give them 22 examples; 	I didn’t tell
them what should be first and what should be second. 	I didn’t do any of that but
they think they’ve got all 	kinds of stuff.
That’s what you want to do 	so 	there’s no boundaries. 	There 	are however needed
bridges and segues 	from one place to another, from what is perceived to be
content to what will now be perceived as call to action.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 37 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	38
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
We do need to help them get from place to place, but you ought to be very clear
that you’re actually moving them from a selling place to another selling place.
You’re not moving them from 	a 	content place to a selling place. 	There 	is no
content place; 	there’s only a content place they think is a content place.
Disney actually does put a movie in every one of
these places and it’s generosity on their part,
because they could just as easily have another
store 	in there and everybody would be just as a
happy by the way.
We’re 	bridging 	and 	segueing 	from 	selling 	to
selling to selling.
Some 	people 	will 	feel 	this 	is 	unfair. 	I 	again
remind you the least fair 	thing in the world is to
get 	somebody 	all 	lathered 	up 	about 	fixing 	a
problem or capitalizing on an opportunity and send them home away from you
lathered up with no means of following through.
What could be less fair than that?
If 	you don’t sell them your thing, 	you wasted their time, so
you’ve 	got to get past any attitudinal things you have about this.
So 	here’s how we do bridges and segues. The 	difference basically is that:
 	A 	segue 	is an uninterrupted transition; it feels completely seamless;
nobody would ever really recognize that you were moving them.
 	A 	bridge 	is a structural device and mostly you are acknowledging,
“Here’s a bridge, 	come with me, we are now going from here to here.”
That’s really the difference; 	the thing you’re accomplishing is the same.
The 	least fair thing
in the world is to get
somebody all
lathered up about
fixing a problem
with no means of
following through.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 38 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	39
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
From End of Idea to Next Idea
So here are some of the best options for 	how to get from the end of an idea to
the next idea.
One, a 	bridge that is 	a 	summary statement plus the introduction of the next idea.
“Now that we have eradicated bad breath, 	if you want 	to 	have a lot of sex we must
also eradicate body odor.”
Now 	we’ve moved from one to
the other 	and 	we’re going to sell
a 	box 	of 	stuff 	that 	includes
mouthwash and deodorant and
soap.
Another bridge structure is trial
close 	that 	their 	response
becomes the reason for B.
“Now that we’ve shown you how
to eliminate bad breath, 	if you
can have great breath all the time, 	wouldn’t you love also to have a wonderful
smelling body so you can actually get laid?” 	“Yes I would.”
Segue 	is a mini story that ties idea A to 	idea B. 	So 	we can tell a story about the
person who now has wonderful breath and still can’t get laid, and he now
discovered actually that, 	the better 	his 	breath smells, 	the more noticeable 	it 	is
that 	his body stinks. 	So we have to fix both these problems.
Segue is a 	mini demo that ties idea A to idea B.
The 	last is a testimonial that ties idea A to 	idea B. So 	the testimonial completes
the loop of the first idea and raises the second idea.
BRIDGES OR SEGUES
How to get from end of idea to next idea 	–
some best options
 	BRIDGE: Summary statement +
Introduction of next 	idea
 	BRIDGE: Trial close, it’s “IF”, the
reason for 	idea “B”
 	SEGUE: Mini-story that ties 	idea “A”
to Idea “B”
 	SEGUE: Mini-demo 	that ties idea “A”
to idea “B”
 	SEGUE: Testimonial that ties idea “A”
to idea “B”
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 39 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	40
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
From One Segment to Next
How to get from one segment to the next, 	you build a bridge of a summary plus a
tease of what’s coming up next.
It’s perfectly fine to say, “Okay that wraps up X, 	now here’s the next amazing
thing 	you’re going to discover.”
“So 	that wraps up how to get big money in big chunks from easy transactions, now
we’re going to talk about how to
multiply 	the 	money 	you 	get
through investing.”
It’s 	perfectly 	okay 	to 	say 	it:
“Okay 	we 	built 	this 	box, 	we
filled it with cool stuff, 	we all
now understand it 	and 	know it.”
What 	you’re 	really 	saying 	is,
“You now know how to build a great marketing message.” 	They don’t, but they’ll
nod and say to themselves they do because I just summarized it and told them
they do.
“Now we’re going to talk about how to figure out who you should deliver the
message to,” 	– 	new 	box. It’s 	fine to do that, 	it’s done a lot.
From Content 	to Commercial
When you’re actually going from content to commercial and it is going to be
noticeable… 	again keep in mind you’re really going from 	perceived 	content to
commercial.
You’re never going from real content to commercial because you shouldn’t be
delivering any real content.
But, if 	it’s going to be seen, felt, recognized, 	then here’s the way 	to 	do it.
BRIDGES OR SEGUES
How to get from one segment to next
BRIDGE: Summary + “tease”/headline 	– 	it’s
perfectly okay to essentially say, “Okay that
wraps up ‘a’ 	– 	now 	here’s the next amazing
thing you’re about to discover: <insert
headline>…proceed with “B”
BRIDGE: Change of scene or format
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 40 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	41
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
Number 	one, 	which is used more often than anything else, 	is a simple reason
why.
The benefit here is the reason why can include an enormous amount of truth.
The 	old version of this was the 	7 to 21 times repetition argument stated one
way or another.
It was some version of 	– 	maybe with the trial close 	– 	“Okay we’ve done this, this
and this and aren’t 	you feeling
great? 	You 	can’t 	wait 	to 	get
home and apply this to your…”
If 	you want, 	do 	a trial close,
“Isn’t that right? 	Everybody 	who
feels that way raise your hand?
Now, 	here’s the bad news.”
Now there’s a thing about how
bad your memory is 	– 	which 	is
terrible.
“X% of 	you three days from now will 	have forgotten everything I said except for
the jokes; 	seven days from now you won’t remember who I was and 14 days from
now you won’t even remember having been here 	– 	let 	alone what the hell we
talked 	about.
Those of you that took a bunch of notes, 	here’s what happens with notes. You 	go
home, 	you’ve 	got a place 	– 	everybody has got a place 	– 	where they put all the notes
from all the seminars they go to.
It’s either under the bed 	or 	it’s out in the garage in boxes; 	it’s in 	those 	nice boxes
you get 	at Office Max 	that you have to assemble yourself with 	the lids.
You’ve 	got a whole stack of them somewhere with all the notes 	that 	came from
seminars, it’s where the notes from this seminar are going to go too.”
BRIDGES OR SEGUES
How to get from “Content to Commercial” /
from Middle 	to Close
1. 	REASON WHY
2. 	“MORE”
3. 	“IF” LIST
4. 	DIRECT &/OR “IN YOUR SERVICE”
5. 	THERE ARE # KINDS OF PEOPLE /
THERE ARE # KINDS OF RESPONSE &
THOUGHT AT THIS POINT
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 41 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	42
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
Therefore 	now we have to solve that problem for people.
The 	way we used to solve it was, “Here’s all the stuff you need to listen to and
you need to listen to it 	7seven to 21 times. Then it will be permanently
embedded in your psychology and you’ll be able 	to…”
We 	don’t tell 	you 	that anymore because if I tell you 	that 	you have to listen to it
7seven to 21 times you don’t buy it.
It’s hard enough to get you to buy it to listen to it
once because you really just want the thing done
for you.
The new hook to that is more the toolkit 	– 	so 	it’s
either tools or it’s done for you.
The solution now to this problem that you’re going leave 	here all lathered up
and you think you’ve 	got it but you don’t 	and, by 	the time you get where you are
that you’re going to try and use it, 	you will not remember how to use it.
If we just taught 	you how to fix your kids, how to have happy, 	healthy, 	respectful
teenagers 	who 	eagerly clean their room and don’t cuss 	at you anymore 	and
here’s the scripts and we showed you… 	by the time you actually get a chance to
use it on the teenager, you won’t remember how to do 	it and 	you’ll bungle it all.
So here’s the 	solution to that.
When we sell “fix the phone,” 	my client Jay Geier, 	we make a big point out of,
“Okay, 	now you’ve 	got it. You 	understand how horrible it is 	and you understand
what a good phone call 	is 	supposed to sound like and all that. But 	if you go
trying to do this yourself, 	here’s what’s going to happen. The staff is going to
either quit or they’re going to kill you, because you will bungle it.”
Sometimes, we’ll tell 	the 	Mike Vance “piss 	in the pool” 	story. (Google 	Vance and
then search for “piss in the pool” if 	you don’t know the story.)
Now, if I tell you that
you have to listen to
it seven to 21 times
you don’t buy it.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 42 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	43
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
One 	way or another, 	we invalidate 	what they think they’re
going to be able to do and now we have a solution.
It used to be spaced 	repetition learning, 	then 	Magnetic Marketing 	is all about a
toolkit that’s 	so brain dead 	that 	you can’t help but use it. 	Just 	take a 	page out,
use it just the way it is 	or fill in a few 	blanks.
If you’re selling 	“done for you,” 	it’s done for you; 	if you’re selling coaching, 	it’s
coaching. But the setup is really the same. So 	there’s a 	“reason why” 	bridge.
Another bridge is the simplest one, 	which used to work a lot, 	is just 	“more.”
“You 	had a good time, 	we had a ball; 	we just did this two-day seminar 	on wealth.
Well guess what. 	There’s a 36-module, 	12-month thing.”
More 	of it, 	you want more of it. 	You 	want to take me home with you, to be with
you night and day don’t you?
So there’s a 	“more” 	argument.
There’s the 	“If” 	bridge. 	I use this a lot when I’m selling. It’s really a series of little
trial 	closes. 	I don’t usually make people raise their hands 	– 	sometimes I do.
There’s the 	“If you have seen great value…” “If you have all gotten at least one
million dollar idea that you’re going to…” 	then there is more. 	So 	there’s the 	“If”
bridge.
There’s the 	“in your service” 	bridge, 	which links to the reason why.
It’s the reason why that I’m about to sell you this stuff is because I am deeply
concerned that you are going to be able to implement, and I will lose sleep and
perhaps over 	time become suicidal 	if I 	think that all I’m doing is getting you
lathered up and leaving you unable to implement. So 	it’s sort of the mission
statement thing.
There’s the 	“divide and conquer” 	bridge which is some version of, “We’ve
reached the point where everybody is going to identify themselves. There are
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 43 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	44
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
really 	X 	kinds of people and they reveal themselves by 	X 	kinds of responses to
where we are at this moment.”
“There’s this kind of people and they are 	already at the back of the room trying
to find the resources. Then there’s 	these kinds of people…”
The 	first time I really saw platform selling 	– 	and 	I saw 	“divide and conquer” 	– 	it
blew me away.
It 	was Mr. 	Gentile Lovable 	Zig Ziglar who did it and it is brutally manipulative. I
was in awe 	and I was very young but I got it instantly.
He’s speaking at a huge 	Amway 	rally… 	now you have to understand multilevel,
everybody recruits somebody else 	– 	that’s the deal.
It’s not like direct selling, where 	maybe there’s two levels. In MLM, 	everybody is
recruiting 	and, 	at the big rallies, 	everybody has got somebody with them 	–
unless they’re 	a 	real loser 	– 	who they are either trying to recruit or they have
just recruited. So 	they’re there with somebody who they are trying to lead.
Zig’s close is, 	“You’ll 	notice, 	if you turn around and look, 	while we’ve been doing
this, 	to facilitate 	the lines 	at the back of the room, 	they have put up three different
color flags.”
And 	they have; 	they’ve put up these flags. 	I watched them do it. They’re the kind
that 	they 	used 	to have 	at the gas stations 	or 	car washes; 	they’re the triangle flags
on a string 	– 	red, 	white and blue.
That’s because there’s three lines back there to get three different packages of
resources.
“You 	want to be in the white line if you’re just kind of messing around with this,
you don’t really know if you’re going to get serious. You’re really kind of a 	“spare
time, 	make a few extra bucks” 	not 	that there’s anything wrong with that. 	You want
to be in the white line and you’re going to get this...”
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 44 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	45
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
He 	said 	it 	differently than me 	but not much. He 	didn’t mention price by the way.
“Now 	if you’re a little more serious and you know that you want to build a business
and you’re going to be an organizational leader, you’re going to help other people
build, 	you want to get in the blue line 	and you’re going to get this…
Now, 	if you’re a real leader and you’re really going to 	lead people to new lives of
prosperity. You’re going to get under the red flag and you’re going to get this…”
So 	exactly who is going to get in the white flag 	line?
What he’s really saying is, “If you have a really, really, really 	tiny dick and you
don’t mind everybody knowing it, 	get in the white line.”
That’s what he’s really saying.
I 	said that to him once; I told him 	this whole story. Having said this, 	I said, “So
my translation of what 	you said is…
He 	said, 	“I would never say that.” Then 	he said, “It probably would have sold a
little more merchandise.”
So 	nobody is going to get in the white line.
Actually this day, 	there were about 	8, 	10 people who got in the white line 	and
they all moved over; 	and a bunch of the people who started in the blue line
moved over.
Because the longest line is the red line. 	How 	can you be there with somebody
you’re supposed to lead and not get in the leader line? 	You’ve 	got to get in 	the
leader line. It’s sales 	by extortion; 	it was magnificent.
I’m a kid; 	I’m watching this; 	I’m thinking, “This is the coolest pickpocketing
scheme. He’s left them no choice with this. This is the greatest thing I’ve ever
seen.”
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 45 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	46
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
I’ve never done it with the lines; 	I’d love to do it with the lines. But you can 	do it
verbally and you can do it as the bridge.
Soften the Bridge
Now, some ways to soften the bridge should you wish to do so.
One 	is 	a 	summary story between the 	Middle and the bridge. 	If 	you look at the
Magnetic Marketing 	speech, 	the 	“Al 	the plumber story” 	is between the 	Middle
and the bridge to a visible commercial, 	to a visible call to action.
The story does several things. 	It 	summarizes all of the things I told them they’re
going to be 	able to do in the
context of one story. 	It’s funny
so it softens the 	transition into
the hard CTA and it sets up the
reason to have the resources.
You 	can 	do 	a 	summary
testimonial between the 	Middle
and the bridge. 	So, instead of a
story, 	it can be a story told about a person which summarizes the main points
you made, and gets us into the bridge or it can be in that person’s voice.
You could show video, 	you could go to a video clip in a webinar of a testimonial
in order to set up the bridge.
You can 	put your mission story here and you can do it as a 	“Blackie” 	story if you
like. Andrew Carnegie 	is a Blackie 	in the 	Napoleon 	Hill story.
This 	could be how you were called out, 	for example, 	on your dereliction of duty;
you were running around 	doing these 	presentations and getting 	a 	standing
ovation, and going on your merry way and relying on people to go figure it out.
Then 	Blackie 	– 	whoever 	he may be, 	the CEO of this big company, 	this highly
successful CEO or this wise elder person who makes 	all the money in the real
BRIDGES OR SEGUES
Some Ways 	to “Soften” 	the Bridge 	or Segue
Into It
 	Signature/Summary Story between
Middle and Bridge
 	Summary Testimonial between
Middle and Bridge
 	A “Blackie Story”
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 46 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	47
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
estate business 	– 	grabbed you by the scruff 	of the neck and took you into a
closet and beat you with a rubber hose, and said to you, “How dare 	you leave all
these people with no resources and no tools?”
You got it and you accepted the responsibility and you went back and you
created all these 	tools so nobody would ever have to struggle unnecessarily
again.
I have a Blackie story I sometimes use; actually 	I have two, 	they really serve the
same purpose.
I was speaking for Continental Foods; they are 	a 	commercial food vendor, 	they
sell to 	restaurants. 	Hilton would 	buy from 	Continental; 	there’s only four 	or 	five
companies in 	the 	business.
So 	I was speaking to Continental Foods southwest region in Phoenix and they
had just got a new 	vice president in sales. 	I 	had 	already been booked 	so 	I’m
speaking and I’m selling 	Magnetic 	Marketing.
You 	can set this up sometimes, but I didn’t set it up; 	the guy spontaneously three
quarters 	way 	through 	the 	commercial
interrupted it 	– 	which is usually not good 	– 	he
said, “Look, 	we feel this is important enough for
everybody to have so 	we’ll pay half. So 	you’ll
only pay half, 	we’ll pay half and, 	if you like, 	we’ll
take it out of your commission checks over the
next 	six 	weeks so you won’t even feel it.”
Then the guy goes 	and sits back down and 	I finish the pitch and people go.
So I’m packing up my stuff, 	everybody has gone to lunch and he comes and says,
“Can we get copies of the order forms?” I 	said, “Of course.”
He said, “Can I get the list right now 	of who 	bought?” I said, “Sure” and he said,
“Because I’m going to fire everybody who didn’t.”
This was his litmus
test to quickly
determine who was
serious about
succeeding.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 47 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	48
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
This was his litmus test to quickly determine who was serious about succeeding
and who wasn’t so he could 	get rid of all the dead wood, without having to
spend six weeks figuring it out.
Now I will use that story to put the fear of God into people about buying.
There’s another version of it about 	Alex Goen 	bringing me 	in 	to do training and
consulting 	at TrimSpa. He 	had assigned the 	“Ultimate Sales Letter” 	book 	to
everybody before I got there, and he popped 	a 	quiz on them before I started.
One 	guy confessed to not having had time to read the book 	and 	Alex said, “Then
you don’t have time to work here either. Out!” 	The 	story serves the same
purpose.
Bridge Examples
Here’s an example of a bridge between idea sections in the Magnetic Marketing
script:
“For the 	sake of our limited time 	[because you want to imply there’s more you
could have given them but you can’t] 	let’s assume we have 	a 	powerful marketing
message 	and 	we’re ready to get it out to the market place…”
So you’ve 	got that now. I showed it to you I taught it to you, you know how to do
it. You’ve 	got a powerful marketing message.
Now we’re going to move onto the next challenge, which is: 	“The world’s greatest
marketing message 	is 	no better than the world’s worst marketing message if it’s
shouted at deaf ears, wasting 	bullets on targets…”
Now we’re 	going to talk about target marketing, so I bridged 	from one to the
other. 	I also made a factually untrue statement that you now have this so we’re
going to go do this. Really what you’ve 	got is an awareness of this and a
knowledge 	that 	you don’t have it. 	That’s really what you’ve 	got.
Here’s another bridge: 	Let’s assume for the sake of our conversation, 	we figured
two things out…
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 48 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	49
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
So 	now I’m going back in case they’ve forgotten 	the first one was message. I’m
telling them its message and… 	the second… 	Now 	here’s the third issue…
“How 	do we take this marketing message 	[implication you’ve already got it] 	that
we have so lovingly 	crafted, birthed 	and delivered 	for 	these prospects we have so
carefully selected?
How do we get it to them? Now we’re going to talk 	about media.
So 	I’ve bridged each time with an untrue statement and the reason is that,
essentially, 	what I’ve said is, “Okay, 	we got your breath fixed but you’re still not
going to get laid because your body stinks. 	So we’ve 	got to move over here now.
For 	this to be any good to you, you also need this.
The script also has a segue 	to the summary story, and it tells them why I’m going
to tell them the story, 	what they should look for in the story 	and 	what the story
is all about. 	“Now I’m going to tell you one final story. This story keeps all 	of 	the
promises I made to you at the beginning of our time together.”
I tell them everything that’s going to be in the story and I give 	them 	the 	specific
instruction, 	“If you take this and you go back through everything we’ve talked
about…” 	So 	it ends with 	a 	specific instruction, 	“Stay tuned; 	pay attention.”
“For 	it to do all of those things, 	all the promises I just restated, every little
nuance 	is important. 	I only have time to tell it once, 	you’ve 	got to listen close.” 	So
there’s a specific instruction there.
Here’s 	the 	problem 	bridge, 	memory 	argument: 	“There 	are 	problems 	with
everything we do 	here 	this afternoon, even if we had twice as much time 20 times
more time. 	I’d like to solve them in seven 	minutes…”
I’m telling them now that the call to action here 	– 	the real commercial 	– 	is 	going
to 	be seven minutes long. 	It 	isn’t seven minutes; it’s longer than seven minutes
but they 	feel better that there’s only seven minutes coming.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 49 of 50 --

CRAFTSMANSHIP 	OF 	ONE TO 	MANY 	SELLING 	PRESENTATIONS 	PAGE 	50
© 2014 GKIC 	Module 3
There’s the 	“if” 	in it: 	“So, if you said maybe, 	then these seven minutes are 	of
concern to you because here are the problems…”
The script has 	got just about everything I just described to you.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 50 of 50 --
